did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Conditional GAN (C'GAN) pipeline to synthesize images for minority classes and a CNN-ViT ensemble architecture (combining ResNet50V2 and DeiT) with an attention fusion module to classify skin cancer lesions from the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a novel two-stage framework using a Conditional GAN pipeline and CNN-ViT ensemble for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology (C'GAN, ResNet50V2, DeiT), datasets (HAM10000), and quantitative results (99.3% accuracy, 99.4% AUC), distinguishing it from a review paper despite containing a literature review section.

title: Enhanced skin cancer classification for minority classes using Conditional GAN pipeline and CNN-ViT ensemble
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of class imbalance and incomplete feature extraction in skin cancer classification by proposing a novel two-stage framework. First, a Conditional GAN (C'GAN) pipeline conditioned on attention maps is used to synthesize images for minority classes within the HAM10000 dataset. Second, a CNN-ViT ensemble architecture combining ResNet50V2 and DeiT is employed, utilizing a cross-attention fusion module to merge local and global features. The model was evaluated using standard quantitative metrics and bootstrap-based statistical analysis to ensure reliability. The proposed approach achieved high accuracy rates of 99.3% for Dermatofibroma, 99.7% for Vascular lesions, 98.9% for Basal Cell Carcinoma, and 98.2% for Actinic Keratosis. Additionally, the framework demonstrated a 99.4% overall AUC and robust stability with a bootstrap mean of 0.93. These results indicate that the method effectively balances performance across both majority and minority classes, overcoming typical limitations of existing deep learning models.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer lesion classes (Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesions, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions using a CNN-ViT ensemble architecture. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories from the HAM10000 dataset. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC for each class, with specific mention of handling class imbalance for minority classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dermatoscopic image dataset consisting of 10,015 samples across seven skin lesion categories. It served as the source for original real images, which underwent hair removal preprocessing and were used to generate synthetic images via a Conditional GAN to address class imbalance.

use.training: Original images were preprocessed (hair removal) and combined with synthetic images to form a balanced dataset used for training the ResNet50V2-DeiT ensemble.
use.validation: Original images were combined with synthetic images to form a balanced dataset used for validation.
use.testing: Original images were combined with synthetic images to form a balanced dataset used for testing.
use.other: Used as the source for generating synthetic images for minority classes using a Conditional GAN pipeline.

datasets_used.name: Balanced Dataset (Synthetic + Real)
datasets_used.description: A custom experimental dataset created by combining hair-removed original images from HAM10000 with C'GAN-generated synthetic images to balance class distribution. This dataset was partitioned into training, validation, and testing sets.

use.training: Used to train the ResNet50V2-DeiT ensemble model with attention fusion.
use.validation: Used to evaluate and fine-tune model parameters and hyperparameters.
use.testing: Used for unbiased evaluation of the final model performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the reliability and stability of the model's performance.
To quantify the variability of the model's performance metrics.
To compute overall performance metrics (Precision, Recall, F1-score) for imbalanced multi-class classification.
To visualize model robustness and class separability via Area Under the Curve (AUC) scores.
To evaluate performance per class and visualize the trade-off between exactness and completeness, particularly under class imbalance.
To depict misclassification patterns and calculate True Positives (TP), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN) for granular performance assessment.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA with p-values) for comparing model performance. It utilized bootstrap-based statistical analysis to estimate reliability and stability, reporting a bootstrap mean of 0.93 and a standard error of 0.0003, but did not perform significance tests between models.

ResNet50V2-MobileNetV2.note: Evaluated in ablation study; lower performance than proposed ensemble.

ResNet50V2-EfficientNetV2.note: Evaluated in ablation study; lower performance than proposed ensemble.

ResNet50V2-MobileNetV2-EfficientNetV2.note: Evaluated in ablation study; lower performance than proposed ensemble.

ResNet50V2-DeiT Ensemble (with Regular A.note: Evaluated in ablation study (Case iv); C'GAN augmentation showed improved results compared to regular augmentation.

HAM10000 (Balanced Dataset with C'GAN Au.notes: The proposed model outperformed other SOTA models in standard evaluation metrics of the minority classes. Models achieving comparable results did not guarantee reliability or consistency across all classes. The confusion matrix showed reduced misclassification between visually similar lesion types.

architecture_used.main_model: ResNet50V2-DeiT Ensemble with Attention Fusion Module
architecture_used.pretraining: ImageNet base-trained weights for both ResNet50V2 and DeiT backbones

architecture_details.backbone_cnn: ResNet50V2 (50-layer deep CNN with bottleneck residual blocks, Batch Normalization and ReLU applied before each convolutional layer, residual connections with final non-linearity removed)
architecture_details.backbone_vit: DeiT (Data-efficient image Transformer variant; patch size 16x16 pixels, linear projection for patch tokens, learnable class token, positional embeddings, Transformer Encoder with Multi-Head Self Attention (MHSA) and Position-wise Feed Forward Networks (FFNs), extra distillation token, simple linear classifier head)
architecture_details.fusion_module: Attention Fusion Module (AFM) inspired by cross-attention mechanism; CNN feature maps pooled to token-like dimension and projected as Key (K) and Value (V); ViT tokens used as Query (Q); Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax((Q*K^T)/sqrt(d_k)) * V; fused features pass through a bottleneck layer for dimensionality reduction
architecture_details.classifier_head: Output layer with Softmax activation for 7-class classification

training_details.training_schedule: Staged training: 1) Backbone networks frozen, fusion layers and classification head trained (warm-up phase); 2) Selected layers of CNN and ViT backbones unfrozen, joint fine-tuning with reduced learning rate; 3) End-to-end training

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal preprocessing technique applied to enable the model to capture lesion characteristics more effectively
Attention maps extraction using Smooth-Grad heat maps from baseline ResNet50V2 to condition the C'GAN generation
Balanced dataset preparation combining original images with C'GAN-generated synthetic images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to generate synthetic images for minority classes to address class imbalance. The C'GAN was conditioned on attention maps (Smooth-Grad heat maps) extracted from a baseline ResNet50V2 model. Specific counts: Dermatofibroma (115 original -> 2385 synthetic), Vascular lesions (142 original -> 2358 synthetic), Basal Cell Carcinoma (514 original -> 1986 synthetic), Actinic Keratosis (327 original -> 2173 synthetic), Melanoma (1113 original -> 2487 synthetic), Benign Keratosis (1099 original -> 2501 synthetic), and Nevus (6705 original -> 95 synthetic). Total balanced dataset size: 24,000 images.
Applied to original HAM10000 images to remove artifacts (hair) and enable the model to capture lesion characteristics more effectively.

Mentioned as a comparative baseline in ablation studies (Case iv), where the CNN-ViT ensemble was evaluated with 'regular augmentation' versus the proposed C'GAN augmentation. Specific geometric or color transform parameters for this regular augmentation were not detailed in the provided context.

conclusions: The proposed two-stage framework, combining a Conditional GAN (C'GAN) pipeline for minority class synthesis and a CNN-ViT ensemble (ResNet50V2-DeiT) with an attention fusion module, effectively addresses class imbalance and incomplete feature extraction in skin cancer classification.

The C'GAN pipeline, conditioned on attention maps, successfully synthesized clinically relevant images for underrepresented classes, moving beyond traditional data augmentation to capture feature diversity.

The CNN-ViT ensemble architecture synergistically merged local spatial features from ResNet50V2 with global contextual features from DeiT, enabling the capture of both fine-grained details and long-range dependencies.
The model achieved high performance on minority classes, with accuracies of 99.3% for Dermatofibroma, 99.7% for Vascular lesions, 98.9% for Basal Cell Carcinoma, and 98.2% for Actinic Keratosis.
The framework demonstrated robust overall performance with a 99.4% AUC, a bootstrap mean of 0.93, and a standard error of 0.0003, indicating high reliability and stability.

Ablation studies confirmed that the proposed ResNet50V2-DeiT ensemble outperformed other CNN ensembles (e.g., with MobileNetV2 or EfficientNetV2) and that C'GAN augmentation provided superior results compared to regular data augmentation.
The model showed balanced performance across both majority and minority classes, reducing false negatives for rare but clinically critical classes, which is vital for diagnostic assistance.

Saliency maps indicated that the model prioritizes relevant lesion regions, with spatially coherent responses that align with clinical plausibility, validating the effectiveness of the attention fusion module.
important_remarks: The manuscript is an unedited 'Article in Press' version; the authors explicitly warn that errors may be present which could affect content validity.
The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting external validation and generalizability to other dermatoscopic populations or imaging devices.
Class balancing was achieved by synthesizing 13,985 synthetic images via C'GAN, resulting in a highly artificial dataset (24,000 total images) that does not reflect natural clinical prevalence.

Minority classes (e.g., Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesions) were augmented to exactly 2,500 samples, potentially introducing synthetic bias and overfitting to generated patterns rather than natural variance.

The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) for statistical significance between models, relying instead on bootstrap mean (0.93) and standard error (0.0003) for stability assessment.

Performance metrics for majority classes (Benign Keratosis, Nevus) are notably lower (F1-scores of 0.90 and 0.92 respectively) compared to minority classes, suggesting the model may be biased toward the augmented minority classes.

The C'GAN conditioning mechanism uses attention maps from a baseline ResNet50V2, creating a potential circular dependency or data leakage risk if the baseline model's biases influence the synthetic data generation.
No external clinical validation or prospective trial data is provided, limiting the immediate clinical relevance and deployment readiness of the proposed framework.
